title: Deep Generative models and Inverse Problems
speaker: Alexandros Dimakis
abstract: Modern deep generative models like GANs, VAEs and invertible flows are showing amazing results on modeling high-dimensional distributions, especially for images. We will show how they can be used to solve inverse problems by generalizing compressed sensing beyond sparsity. We will present the general framework, new results and open problems in this space.

proof_stages.label: High-dimensional distribution models and generative priors
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates unsupervised distribution modeling, reviews Gaussian, graphical-model, and sparsity priors, and introduces deep generative models as differentiable pushforwards of simple randomness capable of representing realistic data.

proof_stages.label: Classical linear inverse problems and sparse compressed sensing
proof_stages.summary: A linear inverse problem y=Ax+noise is ill-posed without structure, and classical compressed sensing supplies that structure through sparsity, l0/l1 recovery, and RIP-type guarantees for random measurement matrices.

proof_stages.label: Replacing sparsity by membership in a generator range
proof_stages.summary: The central algorithmic proposal is to assume x lies in the range of a trained generator and recover it by optimizing over the latent variable z so that AG(z) matches the measurements, using backpropagation through the fixed generator.

proof_stages.label: Recovery guarantees and the generator-restricted embedding condition
proof_stages.summary: For Gaussian measurements, m on the order of k d log n for ReLU generators, or k log L for general Lipschitz generators, suffices so that optimizing in latent space gives pixel error controlled by model approximation error and noise, with the key condition that differences of generated images avoid the nullspace of A.

proof_stages.label: Invertible flows and tractable likelihoods
proof_stages.summary: The second part turns to invertible generative models, where z and x have the same dimension, inversion and likelihood evaluation are tractable, and coupling layers provide trainable invertible volume-preserving building blocks.

proof_stages.label: Hardness of conditional sampling for invertible generators
proof_stages.summary: Even though invertible models can be inverted unconditionally, exact or even coarse approximate conditional sampling for an additive coupling layer would solve NP-hard satisfiability problems, so conditional inverse problems remain computationally hard.

proof_stages.label: Composed-model variational inference for conditional sampling
proof_stages.summary: To get practical conditional samples, a new pregenerator is trained before a fixed pretrained invertible generator so that its latent outputs look Gaussian while the generated images satisfy the measurements, yielding diverse completions and uncertainty estimates.

proof_stages.label: Research program and open problems for generative-model inference
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion frames deep generative models as a third major family of high-dimensional priors alongside sparsity and conditional independence, emphasizing open problems in inversion, training, evaluation, robustness, marginalization, testing, and conditional sampling.
